directions: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Goldie's wood fern is among the largest of the North American native wood ferns and prefers reliably moist but not waterlogged conditions in cool, shaded sites. Dryopteris goldiana is a magnificent, stately, semi-evergreen to deciduous native fern of eastern North American hardwood forests; its large, arching fronds reach 3–4 feet long and are broader than most wood ferns, with a distinctively golden-green color when unfurling in spring that matures to deep, glossy green, and the fronds are nearly as wide as they are long, giving the plant an unusually bold, architectural presence. It is an excellent background fern for shaded borders and naturalizes well under large deciduous trees alongside hostas, astilbe, and Solomon's seal.

Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; soft shield fern is more drought-tolerant than many ferns once established and adapts well to dry shade, making it one of the most garden-worthy of all European ferns. Polystichum setiferum is a beautiful, semi-evergreen to evergreen European fern producing arching, broadly lanceolate fronds with finely textured, soft, lacy pinnae that give it an exceptionally delicate appearance; it has a sprawling, vase-shaped habit with fronds lying nearly flat to the ground in many of the popular cultivars such as 'Herrenhausen' and 'Proliferum', which produce bulbils along the midrib of older fronds and can be used to propagate new plants. It is one of the most ornamental and widely used ferns for shaded woodland gardens in zones 5–9 and pairs beautifully with hellebores, cyclamen, and shade-flowering bulbs.

Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly to strongly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Japanese holly fern tolerates more heat, drought, and deep shade than most ferns, making it one of the most adaptable and reliable ferns for Gulf Coast and Mid-Atlantic shade gardens. Cyrtomium falcatum is an evergreen Asian fern with a bold, coarse texture quite unlike most ferns; its upright to arching fronds are composed of large, glossy, dark-green, holly-like pinnae with a leathery texture that catches light in shade and remains handsome through winter, making it a standout for year-round interest in shaded borders. It is highly deer-resistant, tolerates clay soil better than most ferns, and is an outstanding choice for zones 7–10 as an evergreen groundcover under live oaks and magnolias; in zone 6 it is marginally hardy and may die back in cold winters.

Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, cool, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Braun's holly fern needs more consistent moisture and cooler conditions than Japanese holly fern and is best in northern zones and high-elevation gardens. Polystichum braunii is a beautiful, semi-evergreen to evergreen native fern of cool, moist hardwood and mixed forests of eastern North America, Europe, and Asia; its elegant, narrowly lanceolate fronds are densely covered in silvery-white scales when unfurling in spring, creating a striking pewter effect, and the mature fronds are lustrous, deep green with neatly doubly-pinnate, spiny-tipped pinnae giving it the holly-fern look shared by the genus. It is an outstanding choice for shaded rock gardens, cool woodland borders, and naturalizing under conifers in zones 3–7.

Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Fortune's holly fern is slightly more cold-hardy than Japanese holly fern (C. falcatum) and has more matte, less glossy pinnae with a broader, more rounded shape and a somewhat more erect frond habit. Cyrtomium fortunei is an evergreen Asian fern widely used in zones 6–10 as a bold-textured, low-maintenance shade groundcover; its broadly arching fronds have large, matte to semi-glossy, dark-green pinnae that are slightly less waxy than its close relative C. falcatum and give the plant a subtler, more refined texture that blends well with fine-textured shade companions like Japanese forest grass and creeping Jenny. It is highly deer-resistant, adaptable to clay and dry soil once established, and excellent as an underplanting beneath live oaks, magnolias, and camellias in the Gulf South.

Plant corm divisions or container-grown plants in fall or early spring in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; green dragon prefers conditions similar to jack-in-the-pulpit but is often found in wetter, more open floodplain habitats. Arisaema dracontium is a striking eastern North American native woodland perennial in the arum family closely related to jack-in-the-pulpit; unlike its more famous relative, green dragon has a single large leaf divided into 7–15 elliptic leaflets arranged like a peace sign, and the spathe is greenish, narrow, and inconspicuous but the spadix extends dramatically beyond the spathe tip into a long, whip-like appendage (the "dragon's tongue") up to 6 inches long that is the plant's most distinctive feature. Bright red-orange berries ripen in tight clusters on the stalk in late summer and fall, providing wildlife food but should not be eaten by humans. It is rare in cultivation but worth seeking out for native plant enthusiasts.
